results.title: SuMi TRUST to Partner Side-by-Side with Apollo and Athene as a US$1.5 Billion Investor in a Proprietary Portfolio of Alternative Assets | Apollo Global Management
results.content:
TOKYO and NEW YORK, July 06, 2022 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Holdings, Inc. (“SuMi TRUST Holdings”) and its consolidated subsidiary Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Bank, Limited (“SuMi TRUST Bank”, collectively “SuMi TRUST”) announced a strategic partnership with Apollo (NYSE: APO) in which it has committed US$1.5 billion to invest side-by-side with Apollo and Athene in a proprietary portfolio of diversified alternative assets. With SuMi TRUST’s commitment, Apollo and Athene have $15 billion invested and committed to private market equity alternatives, focused on high-quality, diversified assets. [...] About SuMi TRUST

SuMi TRUST Holdings is the only dedicated trust banking group in Japan, uniquely positioned with its business model duality in trust and banking. SuMi TRUST’s trust business includes asset management and asset administration business, and the banking business serves traditional banking needs of clients, such as lending. SuMi TRUST is No. 1 ranked in many of the trust related business areas, and also focuses on wealth management area. In asset management area, it owns the largest asset management company in Asia and is also ranked No. 1 in asset administration business. SuMi TRUST Holdings’ clientele is broad ranging from pension funds to financial institutions, and provide diverse investment products to these clientele including alternative assets. [...] SuMi TRUST Bank President Kazuya Oyama said, “This is a revolutionary approach to investing in alternatives, with exceptional alignment and diversification. SuMi TRUST Bank is committed to playing a more prominent role in developing the alternative asset market in Japan through this investment. As general life longevity increases and Japan prepares for the ‘100-year life’ era, it is becoming ever more important that investors find asset classes that can generate stable, long-term excess returns. SuMi TRUST Bank seeks to provide investment opportunities that suit the broadening needs of our clients, including private market assets.”

results.title: Sumi style brushes; Anyone have ideas? - WetCanvas: Online Living for Artists
results.content:
I’m playing with some Japanese calligraphy and sumi stylings in a couple of paintings. I have quite a few bamboo brushes with those raggedy bristles that hold so much paint and are kind of floppy.

Anyone have any suggestions on alternative western-style brushes that will get the same affects?

Most of my watercolor rounds don’t hold near as much paint and aren’t quite pointed correctly, I’ve tried several pointed round sables that I have, but they just don’t feel quite right.

Suggestions on good brands for sumi brushes and bristle types?

Thanks :)

:)

You may consider looking at Dagger/Sword Brushes?:) :)

:)

As long as there is light, I will paint it.

## Sumi style brushes; Anyone have ideas?

Home › Forums › Explore Media › Watercolor › The Learning Zone › Sumi style brushes; Anyone have ideas?

I’m playing with some Japanese calligraphy and sumi stylings in a couple of paintings. I have quite a few bamboo brushes with those raggedy bristles that hold so much paint and are kind of floppy.

Anyone have any suggestions on alternative western-style brushes that will get the same affects?

Most of my watercolor rounds don’t hold near as much paint and aren’t quite pointed correctly, I’ve tried several pointed round sables that I have, but they just don’t feel quite right.

Suggestions on good brands for sumi brushes and bristle types?

Thanks :)

:)

Karl [...] :lol:

A good place to start is just a good weasel hair brush and get a feel for it. They handle very similar to a kolinsky, the only difference being sumie brushes are proportioned more like a rigger rather than a round, that’s why they can feel very floppy. Another popular, dirt cheap, and wonderful brush is goat-hare mix. Actually I might recommend this over the weasel hair brush. It’s a core of “purple hair” surrounded by goat hair, generally 70-30 or 50-50, depending on how stiff a brush you want. This is probably the best brush for typical watercolor paintings. Pure goat hair bushes can be iffy, they are softer than squirrel.

results.title: Sumi Ink- Not Just for Calligraphy! – Art Summits
results.content:
You can also use a calligraphy pen plus sumi ink as a substitute for drawing with a pencil or Micron-type marker. The techniques are similar, except that you’ll get more variation in the width of your lines (thanks to the flexible tines of a calligraphy nib.) You’ll also need to be more careful when pushing your pen away from you (like an upstroke when writing calligraphy) so that you don’t catch your nib on your paper.

The gorgeous wreaths below were also created by Annie using a calligraphy pen and sumi ink as part of the Modern Calligraphy Summit®.

Want to learn the styles shown in the images here, or need more guidance from a seasoned artist? Click the image below to learn more about Annie Mertlich’s sumi ink class in the Wildfield Collector’s Pack! [...] This style is definitely different from the traditional Japanese “sumi-e” paintings that you may have seen in the past – but why not teach an old medium some new tricks? 😉

If you’re coming from a watercolor background, sumi ink will be a lovely change from your transparent watercolors. Many people also like to combine the two mediums, by painting with watercolors first, and then adding sumi ink borders or accents.

Ready to give sumi ink painting a shot? Here’s a pro tip: dedicate a brush or two for just sumi ink. It will wash out of your brushes with water, but it can be more difficult if you get the waterproof kind. However, sumi ink is so concentrated that there will likely be sumi remnants in your brush, and you won’t want to dirty up your watercolors with it! [...] Skip to content

Art Tips

# Sumi Ink- Not Just for Calligraphy!

·

Sumi ink is a popular medium for calligraphy, but did you know you can also use it for painting and drawing?

That’s right! When used with a variety of paintbrushes, this affordable, versatile, and rich black ink can create bold, gestural marks; tiny, delicate lines; or even smooth graded washes. And when you use it with your calligraphy pen, you can create so many fun drawings that go beyond just writing.

# Creating Sumi-e (すみえ) for Beginners

By bampandaooin DesignArt

58,125

68

2

Image 3: license

Save PDF

Image 4: Creating Sumi-e (すみえ) for Beginners

Image 5: Creating Sumi-e (すみえ) for Beginners

Sumi-e (墨絵風) also known as suiboku-ga, is Japanese monochrome ink painting or 'ink wash painting'.

 Sumi-e paintings in essence are beautiful forms of art, and they traditionally encompass philosophy as well.

 The origin of sumi-e paintings have roots in Chinese calligraphy, which can be noted by the similar brush strokes. [...] Modern sumi-e includes a wide range of colors and hues alongside the shades of black---and the canvas for the paintings were done on rice paper. Unless you’re really serious about the quality/authenticity of your artwork, or you consider yourself to be a professional in the making, these resources are pricey so there are common papers and paints that can be used to still make good sumi-e.

Specifics: You will need:

 • Canvas (paper), I would recommend thicker paper so the paint doesn’t bleed through

 • Varying brushes, depending on the level of detail and the texture you want (these were commonly made from a combination of bamboo and natural hairs)

 • Felt---sold in fabric stores, cheap (essential for preventing the wet paper from sticking to your underlying surface) [...] In early use of sumi-e, the paintings themselves were viewed as tangible poetry---when together with calligraphy the paintings were often considered visual haiku (haiku is a form of poetry in Japanese literature).

 Sumi-e can be a therapeutic experience---long ago and with the progression of time, Chinese calligraphy has been thought to portray a writer's spiritual attunement.

 With and without the inherent concept of harmony traditionally instilled in sumi-e, the practice itself is generally relaxing, unique, and _fun_!

## Step 1: Be Inspired

results.title: Suminagashi marbling - WetCanvas: Online Living for Artists
results.content:
try to drop the ink softly into the water. i use an eye dropper, or a brush and lay it softly on the the surface to deposit colors. you can’t comb it like regular marbling. i use a cat’s Whisker to gently pattern the rings.

do not use a metal pan. use glass or plastic for the marbling. metal changes the ink reaction some way and it will sink. use an absorbant paper. i use sumi (rice) paper.

the color is softer then traditional acrylic or oil marbled papers but still striking. you are just using plain old water and a few drops of ink so play with it and have fun.

Katydid

Thank you for your information, Katydid. I will look for Bombay inks. I usually shop at ACMoore and Michaels. I haven’t seen many types of inks in either store.

if you have a Hobby Lobby they carry it

Katydid [...] WetCanvas: Online Living for Artists

## Suminagashi marbling

Home › Forums › Explore Media › Mixed Media, Encaustics, Collage and Alternative Materials › Suminagashi marbling

I am interested in the art of Suminagashi marbling paper.(It is the ancient Japanese method of marbling.) I prefer to do ” suminagashi” marbling, because it is done without the thickening agents for the water. I have tried several inks, silk dyes, thinned acrylic paints, and liquid watercolors to float on the water, also using Photo Flo, but either the color sinks, or is just too pale. Does anyone have this method perfected? I would appreciate names of inks &/or paints that will work, and any tips that will help me perfect this method. So anxious to get started, successfully! [...] Beverly McLarty Burrows  
C&C always welcome

I wonder how sumi inks differ from other ones like India ink?

Cyndi L  
  

I wonder how sumi inks differ from other ones like India ink?

The whole naming of these things confuses me. When is ink ink? Some of what is called ink in English isn’t called ink here.. and the difference between the various pigment solutions often seems very subtle.. is there a real difference between watercolour, gouache, and watersoluble ink? They all seem to be variations on a theme, some more fitted for one purpose than another, but they work well together..
